2.note: Behold I send my messenger before your face — Mark combines Mal 3:1 and Isa 40:3; the herald of the LORD's coming is identified with John the Baptist
The voice of one crying in the wilderness: prepare the way of the Lord — Mark's opening fulfillment citation; the comfort passage of Isaiah now identifies John's ministry

9.note: The Spirit descending on him like a dove — the Spirit hovering over the waters at creation; Jesus's baptism as the start of new creation
A voice from heaven: You are my beloved Son with whom I am well pleased — the Servant Song investiture at the baptism; Jesus installed as YHWH's Servant

11.note: You are my beloved Son — Ps 2 royal sonship declaration combined with Isa 42:1 Servant anointing in the baptismal voice

12.note: The Spirit immediately drove him into the wilderness — YHWH led Israel in the wilderness to humble and test them; Jesus undergoes the same wilderness test as the true Israel

13.note: He was with the wild animals and angels were ministering to him — the peaceable kingdom vision (wolf lies with the lamb); the new creation harmony restored in the presence of the true Adam

15.note: The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is at hand — the Danielic judgment-time arrived; the kingdom given to the saints is near in the Son of Man

10.note: The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — the Danielic Son of Man's universal dominion includes the divine prerogative of forgiveness; Jesus exercises it on earth before the throne

17.note: I came not to call the righteous but sinners — the Hosea mercy-over-sacrifice principle; the call of sinners is the mercy that outweighs cultic propriety

23.note: David ate the bread of the Presence when he was hungry — Mark's Sabbath-grain controversy appeal to David; necessity overrides cultic precision in the life of the anointed king

5.note: He grieved at the hardness of their hearts — the hardening of Israel's heart from Isa 6 is what Jesus encounters in the synagogue; the judgment of non-sight/non-hearing is being enacted

27.note: Can the strong man's house be plundered? — YHWH's promise to rescue the prey from the mighty man; Jesus's exorcisms are the plundering of the strong man's (Satan's) house by the stronger one

12.note: So that they may see but not perceive, hear but not understand — Mark applies Isaiah's hardening commission to Jesus's parable teaching; the same divine judgment-mechanism of concealment-by-revelation

35.note: He rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still — YHWH commands the storm and it becomes calm; Jesus exercises the divine sovereignty over the sea declared in Ps 107

41.note: Who is this that even the wind and the sea obey him? — the Psalm describes seafarers who cried to YHWH and he stilled the storm; the disciples' question identifies Jesus as the one the Psalm was about

1.note: The Gerasene demoniac among the tombs — the people who sit in tombs and eat pigs' flesh; the demoniac inhabiting the Gentile graveyard with the unclean animals is the Isaianic portrait of spiritual desolation

25.note: The hemorrhaging woman's 12-year condition — Levitical law on discharges; her condition made her perpetually unclean, unable to participate in community life; Jesus's healing restores her to covenant community

7.note: He sent them out two by two — the Mosaic two-witness requirement applied to mission; the pairs provide mutual accountability and legal testimony for the kingdom's proclamation

34.note: They were like sheep without a shepherd — Ezekiel's indictment of the false shepherds; Jesus sees the crowd with the divine shepherd's compassion, fulfilling YHWH's promise to shepherd his flock himself

41.note: He took the five loaves and blessed and broke them — the manna-in-the-wilderness pattern; Jesus feeds the multitude in the wilderness with miraculous bread, recapitulating the Exodus provision

6.note: This people honors me with their lips but their heart is far from me — Mark's Jesus cites Isa 29:13 against the tradition of the elders; the Isaianic indictment of empty cultic worship repeated in the Pharisaic corban controversy

34.note: Ephphatha: be opened — the healing of the deaf-mute fulfills the Isaianic sign: the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped and the tongue of the mute shall sing for joy

18.note: Having eyes do you not see and having ears do you not hear? — Ezekiel's rebuke of Israel's blindness/deafness applied by Jesus to his disciples; the same prophetic frustration at covenantal non-perception

29.note: You are the Christ — Peter's confession; the Messianic identity rooted in the royal Ps 2 sonship, now spoken in the midst of Gentile territory (Caesarea Philippi)

38.note: The Son of Man will come in the glory of his Father — the Danielic coming with the clouds in the Father's glory; Jesus warns against shame before the one whose coming will vindicate the suffering community

2.note: He was transfigured before them — Moses's face shone after the Sinai encounter; the Transfiguration enacts the Sinai theophany with Jesus as the one whose inner glory is revealed rather than reflected

7.note: Listen to him — the Transfiguration voice adds the Mosaic command to hear the prophet like Moses; Jesus is the definitive prophet whose words are to be heard above all others

12.note: Elijah does come first to restore all things — Jesus confirms the Malachi promise of Elijah's return and identifies John the Baptist as its fulfillment

48.note: Where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched — Mark quotes Isa 66:24 three times (vv.44,46,48) for the final state of those who are cut off; the Gehinnom image from Isaiah's final verse

6.note: From the beginning of creation, God made them male and female — Jesus grounds the divorce argument in the creation order; the maleness-and-femaleness of humanity as God-given is the theological bedrock

8.note: The two shall become one flesh — the creation ordinance of marital union; Jesus cites this as the foundational principle that makes divorce a violation of God's creational intent

45.note: The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many — the Servant who pours out his soul to death for the many; Mark's ransom-saying is the Isaianic Servant's substitutionary purpose applied by Jesus to his own death

9.note: Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD! — the Hallel Psalm of the temple pilgrimage; Mark's crowd recites the messianic entry-Psalm as Jesus enters Jerusalem on a colt

17.note: My house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations — Mark uniquely preserves the for all nations clause; the temple's universal-prayer vocation is the standard violated by the money-changers
You have made it a den of robbers — Jeremiah's temple sermon cited as the second criterion; the temple used as a false security while injustice continues

1.note: A man planted a vineyard — Mark's tenants parable is built on Isaiah's vineyard song; the vineyard is Israel, the tenants are the leaders, the servants are the prophets, the son is Christ

10.note: The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone — Jesus cites Ps 118:22 as his own rejection-and-vindication prophecy; the crucifixion-and-resurrection as the fulfillment of the Psalm

26.note: I am the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — the burning bush self-identification; Jesus argues for resurrection from the present-tense divine self-identification

29.note: Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one — the Shema; the great commandment is grounded in the monotheistic confession before the love-command

36.note: The LORD said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand — David in the Spirit declares the Messiah's exaltation; Jesus uses Ps 110:1 to demonstrate that the Messiah transcends Davidic human descent

14.note: The abomination of desolation standing where it should not be — Mark's Jesus applies the Danielic abomination-of-desolation oracle to the imminent desecration of the temple

24.note: The sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light — the Day of the LORD cosmic signs from Isaiah's oracle against Babylon; applied to the eschatological coming of the Son of Man
Stars falling from heaven — Joel's Day of the LORD description; the same cosmic-sign vocabulary used across the prophetic Day-of-LORD tradition

26.note: The Son of Man coming in clouds with great power and glory — Mark's Olivet Discourse climax cites the Danielic coming-of-the-Son-of-Man vision directly

24.note: This is my blood of the covenant — the covenant-ratification blood of Sinai (Moses threw the blood on the people saying behold the blood of the covenant); Jesus reinterprets the Passover cup as the new-covenant blood
Poured out for many — the Servant's self-pouring for the many (Isa 53:12); the cup-saying identifies the cup with the Servant's vicarious death

27.note: I will strike the shepherd and the sheep will be scattered — Jesus cites Zech 13:7 as the prediction of the disciples' flight at his arrest; the shepherd struck by YHWH's sword

34.note: My soul is very sorrowful, even to death — the Psalmist's soul cast down; Jesus in Gethsemane prays in the language of the Psalms of descent, embodying the suffering righteous one

62.note: You will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming with the clouds of heaven — Mark's most explicit Danielic claim; Jesus combines Ps 110:1 and Dan 7:13 in his response to the high priest

24.note: They divided his garments and cast lots for them — the crucifixion detail fulfilling Ps 22:18; Mark narrates the passion through the Psalmic lens

29.note: Those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads — the mockery of the crucified one mirrors Ps 22:7 (all who see me mock me)

34.note: Eloi Eloi lema sabachthani: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the cry of dereliction quotes Ps 22:1 directly; Jesus prays the great Passion Psalm from the cross

38.note: The curtain of the temple was torn in two — the rending of the veil separating the holy of holies; the barrier to God's presence destroyed by Christ's death

6.note: He has risen, he is not here — you will not abandon my soul to Sheol; the empty tomb as fulfillment of the Davidic Psalm identified in Acts 2 as resurrection prophecy
